slides.title: The Commercial Necessity of Agency Law

content.simple: Imagine you want to buy a house in Lagos while living in London, or you own a big company and can't sign every single receipt yourself. You hire someone to do it for you. This is the heart of agency. In the modern world, business would stop if we couldn't use others to act on our behalf. We use agents because they might have special skills we lack, or they are physically present in places we cannot reach. Essentially, an agent is your 'stand-in' who handles business deals for you so you can focus on other things.
content.academic: The Law of Agency is a foundational pillar of commercial transactions, facilitating the expansion of trade beyond the physical and technical limitations of a single individual. As noted by Robert Lowe, modern commerce would reach a standstill without the utilization of brokers, factors, and auctioneers. The relationship is necessitated by the need for specialized expertise, geographical reach, and administrative efficiency. It creates a framework where a principal can multiply their legal personality through others, ensuring that the complexities of distance and lack of technical skill do not hinder the flow of commercial activities in a globalized economy.
content.exam: Key importance: Facilitates modern commerce by allowing specialization and geographical expansion. Core justifications: 1. Special skill/expertise. 2. Knowledge of local markets. 3. Overcoming distance. 4. Time management for the principal. Robert Lowe notes it is the 'heart of the subject'.

slides.title: Defining Agency and the Conduit Pipe Concept

content.simple: Think of an agent as a 'conduit pipe' or a bridge. The agent connects the Principal (the boss) to a Third Party (the customer). Once the connection is made and the deal is signed, the agent usually steps out of the picture, leaving the Principal and the Third Party in a direct legal relationship. Various legal experts define it as a relationship where one person represents another to change that person's legal position—like signing a contract that binds the boss, not the person who actually held the pen.
content.academic: Agency is a consensual relationship where the Agent is authorized to represent the Principal in dealings with third parties. Fridman emphasizes the agent's power to affect the principal's legal position regarding property or contracts. Adesanya and Oloyede highlight the consensual nature, whether express or implied. Critically, the agent acts as an 'instrumentality' or 'conduit pipe.' Once the agent facilitates a contract between the principal and a third party, the agent typically 'drops out,' leaving the principal and the third party in a state of privity, provided the act was lawful and authorized.
content.exam: Definitions: Fridman (power to affect legal position); Adesanya & Oloyede (consensual relationship). Key metaphor: 'Conduit pipe.' Note: Agency must involve lawful transactions; agents are liable for unlawful acts as joint-tortfeasors.

slides.title: Legal Maxims and Implications of Agency

content.simple: There is a famous Latin rule: 'He who acts through another acts for himself.' This means if you send an agent to buy a car for you, the law treats it as if you walked into the showroom and bought it yourself. Because of this, you get all the rights (like owning the car) and all the responsibilities (like paying for it). Even though you weren't there, the agent's handshake is legally your handshake. This creates a direct link between you and the seller, even though you never met.
content.academic: The fundamental legal implication of agency is encapsulated in the maxim 'Qui facit per alium facit per se.' This principle ensures that the acts of the agent are attributed to the principal, creating reciprocal rights and liabilities. Consequently, agency creates an exception to the traditional doctrine of privity of contract; while the agent negotiates the deal, the resulting legal bond exists strictly between the principal and the third party. This 'alter-ego' status means the principal is bound by the agent's authorized conduct as if the principal had acted personally.
content.exam: Maxim: 'Qui facit per alium facit per se.' Implications: 1. Creates privity between Principal and Third Party. 2. Principal inherits all rights and liabilities. 3. Agent generally incurs no personal liability on the contract.

key_concepts: Qui facit per alium facit per se

content.simple: Agency is built on trust, which lawyers call a 'fiduciary relationship.' It involves two layers: the relationship between the boss and the agent, and the relationship between the boss, the agent, and the customer. Most of the time, both sides agree to this setup, but sometimes the law forces an agency relationship to exist even if they didn't plan it—like in emergencies. Importantly, it doesn't matter what you call it; if the law sees you acting like an agent, it will treat you as one.
content.academic: Agency is characterized by its fiduciary nature, demanding the utmost good faith, honesty, and confidence. It creates a dual structure: a bipartite relationship (Principal-Agent) and a tripartite relationship (Principal-Agent-Third Party). While most agency arises from consent (express or implied), it can also be non-consensual, arising via operation of law through estoppel or necessity. The legal existence of agency is determined by the substance of the relationship and the effects attached to it by law, rather than the labels or intentions chosen by the parties involved.
content.exam: Characteristics: 1. Fiduciary nature (trust). 2. Bipartite and Tripartite structures. 3. Can be consensual or non-consensual (necessity/estoppel). 4. Substance over form (labels don't matter).

slides.title: Commercial Labels vs. Legal Reality

content.simple: In the real world, people use the word 'agent' loosely. For example, a car dealer might call themselves a 'Sole Agent' for Toyota. However, in law, that dealer is often just a buyer and seller. They buy cars from Toyota and sell them to you for their own profit. They aren't 'connecting' you to Toyota; they are acting for themselves. We must distinguish between these commercial 'sole agents' and true legal agents who have the power to bind their principal to a third party. If a contract says someone is a sole agent for a fixed time, the principal cannot just ignore that and start selling directly without consequences.
content.academic: A critical distinction exists between the popular use of 'agent' and its strict legal definition. In commerce, 'sole agents' or distributors often function as independent principals who purchase stock for resale on their own account, rather than acting as conduit pipes. The case of Lamb W.T and Sons v. Goring Brick illustrates this; where a party is appointed as a 'sole selling agent' for a fixed term, the principal may be in breach of contract if they attempt to sell directly, bypassing the agreed-upon commercial arrangement. The court looks at the underlying legal obligations rather than mere terminology.
content.exam: Distinction: Commercial 'Sole Agents' (distributors) vs. Legal Agents. Case: Lamb W.T and Sons v. Goring Brick. Fact: Breach occurred when principal sold goods themselves despite appointing a 'sole selling agent' for a fixed period.

case_references: Lamb W.T and Sons v. Goring Brick (1932)

sections.title: Capacity and Powers of an Agent>
sections.description: Study of Capacity and Powers of an Agent>

slides.title: Introduction to Capacity in Agency Law

content.simple: Think of agency like hiring someone to represent you. For this to work legally, both the person hiring (Principal) and the person being hired (Agent) usually need the legal right to enter contracts. In Nigeria, we follow the basic rules of contract law. This means if you want to hire someone to buy a car for you, you must be legally allowed to buy a car yourself. Also, the person you are buying from (the 3rd party) must be legally able to sell it. If anyone lacks 'capacity'—like being too young or not in their right mind—the deal might not be enforceable in court.
content.academic: The creation of an agency relationship is fundamentally anchored in the general principles of contract law. For an agency to be valid and enforceable, the three parties involved—the Principal, the Agent, and the Third Party—must generally possess the requisite legal capacity to contract. Capacity refers to the legal competence of a person to enter into a binding agreement. While the Principal's capacity determines the scope of what the agent can do, the Agent’s capacity is viewed differently because they act as a representative rather than a primary party to the resulting contract. Failure to establish capacity may render the underlying agency or the subsequent third-party contract void or voidable.
content.exam: Agency follows general contract rules regarding capacity. General Rule: Both Principal and Agent must have capacity for the agency to be fully enforceable. The Third Party must also have capacity for the resulting contract to be valid. Lack of capacity may prevent the enforcement of rights and duties.

slides.title: Capacity of the Principal

content.simple: In Nigeria, a Principal can only give an Agent the power to do things that the Principal is already allowed to do themselves. You cannot bypass your own legal limitations by hiring someone else. For example, if a law says you must sign a document personally, you cannot send an agent. There are two main exceptions: when a law specifically says you must act yourself, or when the job is tied to a specific public office. For companies, they can only appoint agents to do things listed in their 'objects clause' (their business goals).
content.academic: The capacity of a Principal to act through an agent is co-extensive with their own individual capacity to perform the act. A Principal cannot confer upon an agent powers that they do not personally possess. This principle is subject to two primary exceptions: first, where a statute expressly requires personal performance by the individual; and second, where the competence to act arises from holding a specific public office. Furthermore, under Section 39(1) of the Companies and Allied Matters Act (CAMA) 1990, a corporation’s capacity is limited by its objects clause; any appointment of an agent to perform acts outside these objects is considered ultra vires.
content.exam: Rule: Principal's capacity to delegate is co-extensive with their own capacity. Exceptions: 1. Statutory personal performance. 2. Public office duties. Note: CAMA 1990 s.39(1) restricts corporate agents to the company's objects clause (Ultra Vires).

statute_references: Companies and Allied Matters Act 1990, Section 39(1)

content.simple: Children (infants) have limited power. They can only hire agents for 'necessaries' like food, clothes, or education. Any other contract made by an infant's agent is void under the Infants Relief Act. For people who are mentally unwell or drunk, they can only hire agents during 'lucid intervals' (times when they are thinking clearly). If they were incapacitated when the agent was hired, the contract only stands if the other person didn't know they were incapacitated and didn't take advantage of them. If a Principal loses their mind later, the agency usually ends immediately.
content.academic: Infants lack full contractual capacity; thus, they can only appoint agents for 'necessaries' or contracts that are legally binding upon them under the Infants Relief Act 1874. Mentally unsound persons and drunkards have capacity only during 'lucid intervals.' Per Imperial Loan Company v. Stone, a contract made by an incapacitated person is binding unless they prove they did not understand their actions and the other party was aware of the disability. Crucially, the subsequent insanity of a Principal, even if they had capacity at the start, terminates the agency relationship, as demonstrated in Yonge v. Toynbee.
content.exam: Infants: Limited to 'necessaries' (Infants Relief Act 1874). Mentally Unsound/Drunkards: Capacity only during lucid intervals. Voidable if 3rd party knew of disability (Imperial Loan Company v. Stone). Termination: Subsequent insanity ends agency (Yonge v. Toynbee).

content.simple: The rules for Nigerian companies have changed recently to make business easier. Previously, companies were strictly tied to their 'objects clause.' Now, Section 38 of CAMA gives a company the same legal powers as a real human being to carry out its business. This means they have broad powers to hire agents. Section 39 also helps protect outsiders by stating that even if a company acts outside its set goals (ultra vires), those acts aren't automatically void. This ensures that agents representing companies can engage in modern commerce more effectively.
content.academic: The legal landscape for corporate capacity in Nigeria has been significantly modernized. While the ultra vires doctrine historically limited corporate agency, Section 38 of the Companies and Allied Matters Act (CAMA) now grants a company all the powers of a natural person of full capacity for the furtherance of its authorized business. Furthermore, Section 39 largely abolishes the harsh consequences of the ultra vires doctrine. This means that acts exceeding the company's memorandum are no longer ipso facto void, providing greater security for third parties dealing with corporate agents and expanding the functional capacity of the corporation as a Principal.
content.exam: CAMA s.38: Companies have powers of a natural person for authorized business. CAMA s.39: Modernizes ultra vires; acts are not ipso facto void. This significantly broadens corporate capacity to appoint agents compared to common law.

slides.title: Capacity to act as an Agent

content.simple: Surprisingly, an Agent doesn't need full legal capacity! Because the Agent is just a 'middleman' and doesn't usually take on the risks of the contract themselves, even a child (infant) can be an agent. The law treats the Agent as a 'mere instrument' or a tool used by the Principal. As long as the Agent is sane enough to understand the job and agree to do it, the agency is valid. This means a Principal can be bound by a contract made by their underage agent, even if that agent couldn't be sued personally.
content.academic: The rules governing Agent capacity are less stringent than those for Principals. Since an Agent does not personally enter into the contract but merely facilitates a nexus between the Principal and a Third Party, they do not require full contractual capacity. Consequently, infants and others with limited capacity are competent to act as agents. The legal rationale is that the Agent is a 'mere instrument,' and the Principal assumes the risks of representation. The only requirement is that the Agent must possess sufficient understanding to consent to the agency and perform the mandated acts.
content.exam: Agent Capacity: Not required to be full. Rationale: Agent is a 'mere instrument.' Infants/limited capacity persons can be agents. Requirement: Sufficient understanding to consent. Principal remains bound even if the Agent lacks personal contractual capacity.

content.simple: Usually, you don't need a written contract to start an agency. You can appoint someone by talking to them (oral), writing to them, or it can even be assumed from how you both act. However, if you want an agent to sign a 'Deed' (a very formal legal document), you must appoint them using a 'Power of Attorney,' which is also a deed. If there is a dispute, the person claiming that an agency exists must prove it. Remember: just staying silent doesn't count as an agreement; there must be some clear action or word.
content.academic: Generally, no specific formalities such as writing or a deed are required for the valid appointment of an agent; agency may be created orally, in writing, or implied from circumstances. However, the 'equal dignity rule' dictates that if an agent is authorized to execute a deed, their appointment must also be under seal (a Power of Attorney). In Abina & Ors v. Farhat, it was held that a deed signed by an agent with only verbal authority was inoperative. While implied agency is possible, it requires a positive act; mere silence is insufficient. The burden of proof rests on the party asserting the agency relationship.
content.exam: General Rule: No formalities required (oral/written/implied). Exception: Appointment by Deed (Power of Attorney) required for agents executing deeds. Case: Abina & Ors v. Farhat. Proof: Burden lies on the assertor; silence is not consent.

sections.title: Duties and Rights in an Agency>
sections.description: Study of Duties and Rights in an Agency>

slides.title: Introduction and the Duty to Perform the Undertaking

content.simple: When you agree to be someone's agent, your first job is to actually do what you said you would do. Think of it like a friend asking you to buy a specific phone for them because you're good at finding deals. If you're being paid, you must do it or you've broken a contract. If you're doing it for free (a gratuitous agent), you aren't forced to start, but once you actually begin the task, you must finish it properly. You can never be forced to do something illegal, even if you were paid to do it.
content.academic: The relationship of agency imposes a primary obligation on the agent to perform the undertaking. Under Nigerian commercial law, a contractual agent is liable in damages for non-performance of the agreed mandate. While a gratuitous agent is generally not liable for a total failure to perform (non-feasance), the rule in Coggs v. Bernard establishes that once performance commences, the agent is bound by the same standards as a paid agent. Notably, no duty exists to perform an illegal act, as such contracts are void ab initio.
content.exam: Agent must fulfill the mandate. Contractual agents are liable for non-performance. Gratuitous agents: no liability for non-performance unless they commence the act (Coggs v. Bernard). Illegal instructions are unenforceable.

slides.title: The Duty of Obedience to Instructions

content.simple: An agent must follow the principal's instructions exactly. Even if you think the principal is making a bad choice that will lose them money, you must obey if the instruction is clear and legal. It's like a driver being told to take a specific route; even if they know a shortcut, they should follow the boss's path. If the instructions are confusing or 'vague', you should try to contact the principal, but you can use your best judgment to help them if you can't reach them.
content.academic: The duty of obedience requires an agent to comply strictly with the express and lawful instructions of the principal. In Fray v. Voules, it was established that an agent must obey even if the act appears detrimental to the principal. However, where instructions are ambiguous, the agent may act in the principal's best interest. Professional agents are further constrained by the ethics of their profession; they cannot be compelled to perform acts that violate professional codes of conduct.
content.exam: Strict compliance with lawful instructions is mandatory (Fray v. Voules). Ambiguous instructions allow for reasonable interpretation. General words are limited by specific descriptions (Jacob v. Morris).

case_references: Nigerian Craft Bags Ltd v. Express Clearing and Shipping Nigeria Ltd

slides.title: The Duty of Care and Skill

content.simple: When doing a job for a principal, you must be careful and show you know what you're doing. If you're a professional (like an estate agent), you're expected to have the skills of a normal professional in that field. If you're just a friend helping out, you just need to be as careful as a normal person would be with their own stuff. You don't have to guarantee a perfect result, but you must try your best and act honestly.
content.academic: Agents owe a duty to exercise requisite skill and diligence. The standard of care varies: a contractual agent must exhibit the skill expected of a person in their specific profession. A gratuitous agent must exercise the care a reasonable man would use in his own affairs. Per Omotayo v. Ojikutu, an agent does not guarantee success but must act honestly and with reasonable competence. In sales, this includes an ongoing duty to secure the best possible price for the principal.
content.exam: Contractual agents: professional standard. Gratuitous agents: reasonable man standard. No guarantee of success, only honesty and reasonable skill (Omotayo v. Ojikutu).

slides.title: Non-Delegation: Delegatus Non Potest Delegare

content.simple: The principal chose you because they trust you personally. Therefore, you generally cannot pass your job to someone else. This is called the rule against delegation. However, there are times you can get help: if the task is a simple 'office' task that doesn't need special skill, if it's normal in that business to use helpers, if there's an emergency, or if the principal says it's okay. Usually, if you hire a helper (sub-agent) without permission, they are your responsibility, not the principal's.
content.academic: The maxim 'delegatus non potest delegare' reflects the confidential nature of the agency relationship. Since the principal relies on the agent's personal qualities, the agent must generally act personally. Exceptions exist for ministerial acts, trade usage, unforeseen emergencies, or express authorization. Under Calico Printers Association v. Barclays Bank, a sub-agent typically lacks privity with the principal and is responsible only to the agent, unless the principal specifically authorized the creation of a direct agency relationship.
content.exam: Maxim: Delegatus non potest delegare. Exceptions: Ministerial acts, custom, emergency, consent. No privity between principal and sub-agent unless authorized (De Bushe v. Alt).

case_references: Calico Printers Association v. Barclays Bank

slides.title: Fiduciary Duties: Good Faith and Secret Profits

content.simple: Agency is built on 'utmost good faith.' This means you must be 100% loyal and honest. You are not allowed to make 'secret profits'—extra money you made from the deal that the principal doesn't know about. This includes kickbacks or bribes. If you take a bribe or hide a profit, you have to give that money to the principal, and you might lose your right to be paid for your work. It's about ensuring you don't put your own pockets ahead of the principal's interests.
content.academic: The fiduciary nature of agency demands 'uberrimae fidei' (utmost good faith). Agents are strictly prohibited from making secret profits, which include undisclosed rebates, commissions, or bribes. In Palmer Nig. Ltd v. Fonsela, it was affirmed that any secret profit must be disgorged to the principal. Acceptance of a bribe is treated with particular severity, often resulting in the forfeiture of the agent's remuneration and potential criminal liability, as it constitutes a fundamental breach of the duty of loyalty.
content.exam: Duty of Good Faith (Uberrimae Fidei). No secret profits or bribes. Breach leads to forfeiture of commission and refund of profits (Palmer Nig. Ltd v. Fonsela).

sections.title: Termination of an Agency>
sections.description: Study of Termination of an Agency>

slides.title: Introduction to the Termination of Agency

content.simple: Think of an agency relationship like a contract or a job. It starts when two people agree to work together, and it ends when that agreement stops or when something happens that makes it impossible to continue. In Nigerian law, we group the ways an agency ends into two main buckets: 'Acts of the Parties' and 'Operation of Law.' The first bucket is when the people involved decide to stop, like quitting or being fired. The second bucket is when the law automatically steps in to end the relationship because of an event like death or a change in the law.
content.academic: The termination of an agency relationship signifies the formal cessation of the agent's authority to alter the legal position of the principal. In the Nigerian commercial legal framework, this dissolution is categorized into two primary modalities: termination by the acts of the parties and termination by operation of law. Termination by acts of the parties involves consensual or unilateral decisions, such as mutual agreement, revocation by the principal, or renunciation by the agent. Conversely, termination by operation of law occurs automatically upon the occurrence of specific legal events, such as death, insanity, or bankruptcy, which fundamentally extinguish the legal capacity or the subject matter of the agency relationship.
content.exam: Termination occurs via: 1. Act of Parties (Mutual agreement, revocation, or renunciation). 2. Operation of Law (Death, insanity, bankruptcy, frustration, or subsequent illegality). Essential for determining the subsistence of 'apparent authority' and the liability of the principal to third parties.

slides.title: Termination by Mutual Agreement and Performance

content.simple: The easiest way for an agency to end is if everyone agrees to it. If you hire someone to sell your car and you both decide to cancel the deal, the agency ends by 'mutual agreement.' Also, if the job is finished, the agency ends naturally. For example, if you hire an agent specifically to sell a house, once the house is sold and the money is paid, the agent no longer has any power to act for you. Similarly, if you hired them for just three months, the relationship ends automatically once those three months are up.
content.academic: Agency is fundamentally a consensual relationship; hence, it may be determined through bilateral agreement. Where an agency is created for a specific venture or a fixed duration, authority terminates automatically upon the fulfillment of that purpose or the effluxion of the stipulated time. This is known as termination by performance or lapse of time. In these instances, the legal substratum of the agency is exhausted, and the agent's actual authority ceases to exist without the necessity of further formal notice between the principal and the agent, as the terms of the initial agreement have been satisfied.
content.exam: 1. Mutual Agreement: Bilateral termination. 2. Completion of Purpose: Agency ends once the specific task is accomplished. 3. Effluxion of Time: Automatic termination upon expiry of a fixed period. No further notice is required between parties when the term expires.

content.simple: Sometimes only one person wants to end the relationship. If the principal (the boss) cancels the agent's power, it is called 'revocation.' If the agent decides to quit, it is called 'renunciation.' However, there is a big rule: the principal must tell the public or the customers that the agent is gone. If the principal fires the agent but doesn't tell anyone, and the agent goes ahead to sign a deal with a customer who doesn't know, the principal might still be forced to honor that deal because the customer acted in good faith.
content.academic: A principal may unilaterally revoke an agent's authority, while an agent may unilaterally renounce it. While this terminates 'actual' authority, the principal must provide express notice to third parties to extinguish 'apparent' or 'ostensible' authority. Failure to notify third parties who have previously dealt with the agent may result in the principal being bound by subsequent contracts under the doctrine of holding out. It is important to note that revocation or renunciation does not retroactively affect rights or liabilities already accrued between the principal and third parties prior to the effective notice of termination.
content.exam: 1. Revocation: Unilateral act by principal. 2. Renunciation: Unilateral act by agent. 3. Notice Requirement: Crucial for third parties to prevent 'apparent authority' issues. 4. Exception: Irrevocable agencies (authority coupled with an interest) cannot be unilaterally revoked.

content.simple: If either the principal or the agent dies, the agency relationship dies with them immediately. Even if the agent doesn't know the principal has died and continues to work, they cannot claim their salary for that work. In fact, the agent could be in trouble and might be sued by customers for pretending to still have authority. The same rule applies to insanity. If the principal loses their mind, the agent's power to sign deals usually stops, though there are some protections for customers who didn't know about the insanity.
content.academic: Under Nigerian law, the death or insanity of either party terminates the agency relationship by operation of law. Upon the death of the principal, the agent's authority ceases instantly, regardless of whether the agent has notice of the event. Consequently, the agent cannot maintain an action for remuneration or indemnity for acts performed post-mortem. Furthermore, an agent acting after the principal's demise may be liable to third parties for 'breach of warranty of authority.' Regarding insanity, the agency terminates automatically unless it involves an irrevocable power of attorney, though third-party rights may be protected if they lacked knowledge of the condition.
content.exam: 1. Death: Automatic termination; notice is irrelevant to the cessation of actual authority. 2. Insanity: Terminates agency (except irrevocable types). 3. Liability: Agent may be liable for breach of warranty of authority if they continue to act. Case: Drew v. Nunn.

content.simple: There are three other legal ways an agency ends. First is bankruptcy: if the agent goes broke, they usually lose the right to act for the principal. Second is 'subsequent illegality': if the government passes a law that makes the agent's job illegal, the relationship must stop. Finally, there is 'frustration.' This happens when an unexpected event makes it impossible to continue, like if the agent was hired to sell a house, but the house is destroyed by a fire before it can be sold. Because the house is gone, the agency is frustrated and ends.
content.academic: Termination by operation of law also encompasses bankruptcy, subsequent illegality, and frustration. The bankruptcy of an agent typically terminates the relationship as it impacts their legal capacity to conduct commercial transactions. Subsequent illegality occurs when a change in legislation or a state of war renders the agency's objective or the continued relationship unlawful. Frustration applies when an unforeseen, extraneous event—such as the destruction of the subject matter—renders the performance of the agency impossible or radically different from what was contemplated. These events discharge the parties from future obligations by force of law, effectively dissolving the agency relationship.
content.exam: 1. Bankruptcy: Terminates the relationship (usually of the agent). 2. Subsequent Illegality: Agency ends if the purpose becomes unlawful. 3. Frustration: Termination due to destruction of subject matter or impossible performance. Result: Automatic discharge of future obligations under the agency.

content.simple: Think of hire purchase as a 'rent-to-own' system. Imagine you want a car but cannot afford the full price today. You enter an agreement with the owner to take the car home and pay a monthly fee called 'rent.' Here is the catch: you are just hiring the car at first. You only become the owner after you have paid all the installments and a tiny final fee called an 'option to purchase.' Because you are not yet the owner, you cannot sell the car to someone else while you are still paying for it. This system protects the seller from people who might try to sell the car before finishing their payments.
content.academic: A contract of hire purchase is a hybrid legal construct consisting of a bailment of goods coupled with an option to purchase. Under this arrangement, the owner (letter) grants possession of the chattel to the hirer in exchange for periodical payments known as hire-rent. Crucially, the proprietary interest remains vested in the owner throughout the subsistence of the hiring period. Ownership only translates to the hirer upon the fulfillment of two conditions: the completion of all stipulated installments and the subsequent exercise of an 'option to purchase,' often involving a nominal fee. This distinguishes the transaction from a standard sale, as the hirer is under no legal obligation to buy but merely possesses a right of election.
content.exam: Hire Purchase is a bailment of goods where the hirer pays installments with an option to purchase. Key elements: 1. Possession passes, not ownership. 2. Hirer has an option, not an obligation, to buy. 3. Title passes only upon full payment and exercise of the option. It avoids Section 9 of the Factors Act 1889/Sale of Goods Act provisions regarding buyers in possession passing good title.

slides.title: The Landmark Case: Helby v Mathews

content.simple: In this famous case, a man hired a piano. He agreed to pay monthly installments and had the choice to return the piano anytime or keep it until it became his. However, before he finished paying, he took the piano to a pawn shop to get a loan. The court had to decide: could the pawn shop keep the piano? The House of Lords said 'No.' Because the man had the right to return the piano, he hadn't 'agreed to buy' it yet. He was just a hirer. Therefore, he couldn't give the pawn shop a legal right to the piano because he didn't own it yet.
content.academic: The case of Helby v Mathews [1895] AC 471 serves as the locus classicus for distinguishing hire purchase from an agreement to sell. The House of Lords scrutinized an agreement involving a piano where the bailee had the power to terminate the hiring by returning the instrument. The court held that because the bailee was under no legal obligation to complete the purchase, he was not a person who had 'bought or agreed to buy' goods within the meaning of the Factors Act. Consequently, his pledge of the piano to a third party was invalid against the true owner. This case established that the essence of hire purchase is the absence of a binding obligation to purchase.
content.exam: Helby v Mathews (1895): Established that a hire-purchase agreement is not an 'agreement to buy' because the hirer can return the goods. Thus, the hirer cannot pass a valid title to a third party (nemo dat quod non habet). This protects the owner's interest against unauthorized dispositions by the hirer.

slides.title: Hire Purchase vs. Sale and Credit Sale

content.simple: It is easy to confuse hire purchase with a 'credit sale.' In a credit sale, you buy the item immediately and owe the money as a debt. You own the item from day one. If you stop paying, the seller can sue you for the money, but they usually cannot just take the item back because it is yours. In hire purchase, you don't own it until the very last payment. If you stop paying, the owner can often repossess (take back) the item because it still belongs to them. One is a sale on credit; the other is a hire that might become a sale later.
content.academic: The fundamental distinction between a sale and hire purchase lies in the legal obligation of the parties. In a sale or credit sale, ownership transfers to the buyer either immediately or upon a condition, but the buyer is legally committed to the purchase. If a buyer defaults in a credit sale, the seller's primary remedy is an action for the price. Conversely, in hire purchase, the hirer has a mere option. As seen in G.B. Ollivant v Alansanya, if an owner repossesses a vehicle due to default, they have satisfied their claim and cannot generally sue for the remaining purchase price as if it were a debt, because the contract was one of hire, not sale.
content.exam: Sale: Legal obligation to buy; ownership passes early; remedy is action for price. Hire Purchase: Option to buy; ownership passes at the end; remedy includes repossession. Credit Sale: Ownership passes immediately, but payment is deferred. Conditional Credit Sale: Obligation to buy exists, but title is retained by the seller until payment (distinguished from HP by the lack of an option to terminate).

slides.title: Statutory Definition and Essential Features

content.simple: Nigerian law has a specific rulebook for this called the Hire Purchase Act of 1965. It defines hire purchase as a 'bailment'—which is just a legal word for letting someone hold your property for a reason. The law says that for a contract to be a hire purchase, it must be clear that the person holding the goods 'may' eventually own them. If the contract doesn't give the hirer the choice to buy the goods at the end, it is just a simple rental (like hiring a taxi), and the rules of hire purchase won't apply.
content.academic: Section 20 of the Hire Purchase Act 1965 defines the transaction as 'a bailment of goods in pursuance of an agreement under which the property in the goods will or may pass to the bailee.' The Act also addresses complex 'split' agreements, stating that if two or more agreements together result in a bailment with an option to buy, they are treated as a single hire purchase agreement. Essential features include: 1. It is a form of bailment. 2. Possession is transferred while ownership is retained. 3. The hirer possesses an option, not an obligation, to purchase. Without this option, as in J. Allen & Co v Sanni Adewale, the contract fails as a hire purchase agreement.
content.exam: Section 20 HPA 1965: Defines HP as bailment where property 'will or may pass.' Essential features: 1. Bailment (possession). 2. Option to purchase (essential for HP). 3. Proprietary interest stays with the owner. Case: J. Allen & Co v Sanni Adewale (No option to buy = No hire purchase).

case_references: J. Allen & Co v Sanni Adewale

content.simple: Hire purchase is different from other ways of getting goods. In a simple 'Hire' (like renting a generator for a party), you never intend to own it; you just pay for the use. In a 'Mortgage' or 'Loan,' you might borrow money from a bank to buy a car outright. The car is yours, but the bank has a legal 'hook' on it as security. If you don't pay the bank back, they can take the car. These loan-based deals are often governed by the Bill of Sales Act, which is much more complicated and less popular for everyday consumers than hire purchase.
content.academic: Hire purchase must be distinguished from simple hire, where no option to purchase exists and the owner may repossess after the term. It also differs from Loans and Mortgages. In a mortgage scenario, a buyer may purchase goods outright using a loan from a third-party finance house, then mortgage the goods back as security. Such transactions are subject to the Bill of Sales Act, which imposes strict formal requirements. Hire purchase is commercially preferred in Nigeria because it avoids the complexities of the Bill of Sales Act while providing the owner with the security of retained ownership until full payment is received.
content.exam: HP vs. Hire: Hire has no option to buy. HP vs. Mortgage: Mortgage involves a loan and security interest (Bill of Sales Act applies). HP vs. Pledge: Pledge involves delivery of goods as security for a debt, not for use with an intent to eventually own. Nigerian commercial practice favors HP for its security and relative simplicity compared to Bill of Sales requirements.

slides.title: Definition and Nature of a Contract

content.simple: Think of a contract as a serious promise that the law will help you enforce. It is not just a casual agreement between friends; it is a deal where both sides have specific duties they must fulfill. If you agree to paint a neighbor's house for a fee, you have both created legal rights and duties. One person gives up something (like money) to get a benefit (a painted house). In Nigeria, judges like Tobi, J.C.A. have explained that a contract is simply an agreement that creates a 'reciprocal' or 'back-and-forth' obligation to do or not do something specific.
content.academic: A contract is defined as a legally binding agreement between two or more parties that creates reciprocal legal obligations. According to Tobi, J.C.A., it is an agreement which creates duties to perform or abstain from specific acts. While most contracts involve a mutual exchange of benefits, the core requirement is the intention to affect the legal rights and duties of the parties involved. The law intervenes to enforce these obligations to prevent the disruption of commercial life. A party may suffer a legal detriment—such as forgoing a legal right—in exchange for a benefit conferred by the other party, forming the basis of the contractual relationship.
content.exam: Definition: A legally binding agreement creating reciprocal obligations. Per Tobi J.C.A.: An agreement between two or more parties to do or not do particular things. Key elements: Legal bindingness, reciprocal duties, and enforcement by the court.

slides.title: The Rule of Parties and Commercial Rationale

content.simple: In the eyes of the law, every contract really only has two 'sides' or parties, even if many people are involved. For example, in a loan guarantee where a bank lends money to a student and a parent promises to pay if the student fails, there are actually two separate contracts happening at once. Why does the law care so much? Because our modern economy depends on people keeping their word. If everyone could break their business promises without any punishment, buying things or running a shop would become impossible and confusing for everyone.
content.academic: The doctrine of privity suggests that a contract involves two parties; where more appear to exist, the law typically identifies multiple distinct contracts. For instance, a contract of guarantee involving a lender, debtor, and guarantor consists of two separate agreements: the primary loan contract and the collateral guarantee. The enforcement of these obligations is rooted in economic necessity. Modern commercial life relies on the predictability of promises. Allowing parties to breach agreements without repercussions after another party has relied on them would cause significant economic disruption, necessitating a legal framework for enforcement.
content.exam: Parties: Strictly bipartite; complex arrangements (e.g., guarantees) involve multiple contracts. Rationale: Ensuring commercial stability and preventing disruption caused by reliance on broken promises.

content.simple: Contracts are grouped into two main types based on how official they look. 'Formal contracts' (also called deeds) must be in writing and signed—traditionally they even needed a wax seal! Because they are so official, they don't always need a 'price' or exchange of value to be valid. 'Simple contracts' are everything else. They can be written or just spoken (oral). The most important rule for a simple contract is that there must be 'consideration,' which means both people must bring something to the table, like money or a service.
content.academic: Contracts are classified into formal and simple categories. Formal contracts, or deeds, require specific formalities: they must be in writing and executed under seal (or signed under modern practice). As established in Xenos v. Wickham, the intention to be bound is paramount. Crucially, formal contracts do not require consideration to be enforceable. Conversely, simple contracts include all other agreements, whether oral (parol) or written. As held by Skynner, C.B. in Rann v. Hughes, even a written contract is 'simple' if not under seal, meaning it remains unenforceable unless the plaintiff has furnished consideration.
content.exam: Formal: Deeds/Under seal; no consideration required (Xenos v. Wickham). Simple: Oral or written; requires consideration (Rann v. Hughes). Parol: Historically both oral/written, now mostly refers to oral.

content.simple: An 'express' contract is one where everyone clearly states the rules, the price, and the timing. If you sit down and agree to buy a house for 10 million Naira by December, that is express. An 'implied' contract is different—it happens through your actions. Think about boarding a bus. You don't usually talk to the driver about the price before sitting down, but by getting on, the law says you have 'implied' or signaled that you will pay the fare in exchange for the ride.
content.academic: Contracts are 'express' when terms are explicitly articulated during negotiations regarding price, duration, and subject matter, as seen in Mountford v. Scott. In contrast, an 'implied' contract is inferred by the court from the parties' conduct rather than their words. A classic example is a passenger boarding public transport, creating an unspoken agreement to pay for the service. In Brogden v. Metropolitan Railway Co., the court held that despite the lack of a signed document, the parties' long-term adherence to the terms of an unsigned agreement created a binding implied contract through their conduct.
content.exam: Express: Terms clearly stated (Mountford v. Scott). Implied: Inferred from conduct/actions (Brogden v. Metropolitan Railway Co.). Key: Mutual assent shown through performance.

slides.title: Bilateral and Unilateral Contracts

content.simple: A bilateral contract is a 'promise for a promise.' If I promise to sell you my car and you promise to pay me next week, we are both committed even before the money moves. A unilateral contract is a 'promise for an act.' The most famous example is a reward poster. If a company offers 100 Naira to anyone who uses their medicine and still gets sick, they aren't asking for your promise to use it. They are making an offer to the whole world, and you 'accept' the deal simply by doing what the ad says.
content.academic: Bilateral contracts consist of a mutual exchange of promises where consideration is 'executory'—yet to be performed. An example is an agreement to build a house in exchange for future payment. Unilateral contracts involve a promise made in exchange for a completed act (executed consideration). These are often 'offers to the world' (ad hoc). In the landmark case of Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co., the court held that the plaintiff’s act of using the product as directed constituted valid acceptance of the company's offer, and no prior communication of acceptance was necessary.
content.exam: Bilateral: Exchange of promises; executory consideration. Unilateral: Promise for an act; executed consideration. Key Case: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (Acceptance by performance).

case_references: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co.

sections.title: Formation of a Contract>
sections.description: Study of Formation of a Contract>

slides.title: The Five Pillars of a Valid Contract

content.simple: Think of a contract like a building; it needs specific pillars to stand. In Nigeria, there are five main ingredients needed to make a contract legally binding. First, someone must make a clear 'Offer.' Second, the other person must say 'Acceptance.' Third, there must be 'Consideration' (usually money or something of value). Fourth, both people must actually 'Intend' for the deal to be a legal one, not just a joke or a social favor. Finally, they must have the 'Capacity' or the legal power to enter the deal (like being of age). If any of these are missing, the contract might not exist at all.
content.academic: The formation of a valid contract in Nigerian law requires the concurrence of five essential elements. Traditionally, the common law recognized four: offer, acceptance, consideration, and the intention to create legal relations. However, Nigerian jurisprudence, specifically per Niki Tobi JCA in Orient Bank v. Bilante Intl, has established 'Capacity to Contract' as a critical fifth element. All five components must coexist for a contract to be enforceable. While parties often enter agreements without consciously contemplating legal intent, the court will objectively assess whether these elements are present to determine if a binding obligation has been created between the promisor and the promisee.
content.exam: Key Elements: Offer, Acceptance, Consideration, Intention to Enter Legal Relations, and Capacity. Leading Authority: Orient Bank v. Bilante Intl. Note: Capacity was explicitly identified as the fifth element by Niki Tobi, JCA.

content.simple: An offer is more than just a conversation; it is a serious promise. Imagine telling a friend, 'I will sell you my phone right now for 50,000 Naira.' That is an offer because it is clear and ready to be finished the moment your friend says 'Yes.' If your statement is vague, like 'I might sell my phone later,' it is not an offer. In Nigerian law, the person making the offer (the offeror) must be ready to be legally stuck with the deal the second it is accepted. If there is any doubt about the terms, the court will likely say no offer was made.
content.academic: An offer is defined as a definite undertaking or promise made by the offeror with the intention that it shall become binding as soon as it is accepted by the offeree. Precision is paramount; the terms must be unequivocal, leaving no room for ambiguity. In David Ejinyi v. Amusa Adio, the court held that a mere claim of repurchase without proof of a clear agreement to sell did not constitute a contract. Similarly, in Olaopa v. Obafemi Awolowo University, preliminary drawings and sketches submitted by an architect were deemed insufficient to constitute a formal offer, as they lacked the requisite finality and intent to be bound.
content.exam: Definition: A definite undertaking with intent to be bound upon acceptance. Requirements: Must be certain and communicated. Cases: David Ejinyi v. Amusa Adio; Olaopa v. Obafemi Awolowo University.

slides.title: Communication: Express, Implied, and General Offers

content.simple: Offers don't always have to be written down or spoken clearly. They can be 'Express' (using words) or 'Implied' (shown through actions). For example, when a bus stops at a designated bus stop in Lagos, it is making an 'implied offer' to transport passengers. You accept by getting in. Also, an offer can be made to one specific person or to the 'whole world.' If a company advertises a reward to anyone who finds their lost dog, they have made an offer to everyone, and the first person to find the dog creates a contract by doing the task.
content.academic: Offers may be categorized by their mode of communication: express or implied. Express offers are stated in clear terms, while implied offers are inferred from conduct, as seen in Brodgen v. Metropolitan Railway Co. In the Nigerian case of NNSC v. Agricor Incorporations of USA, the court affirmed that an offer could be accepted impliedly through the delivery of goods and payment. Furthermore, Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. established that an offer can be made to the public at large (unilateral contract), where performance of the condition constitutes acceptance and creates a binding obligation without prior notification to the offeror.
content.exam: Modes: Express (words) vs. Implied (conduct). Unilateral Offers: Made to the world. Cases: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (General Offer); NNSC v. Agricor (Implied Offer); Brodgen v. Metropolitan Railway Co.

case_references: Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. v. Carlill (1893)
NNSC v. Agricor Incorporations of USA

content.simple: Not every 'for sale' sign is an offer. Many things are actually 'Invitations to Treat.' This is like an invitation to start negotiating or an 'offer to receive offers.' The big difference is that you can't be forced into a deal from an invitation to treat. For example, if a company sends a letter saying they plan to let employees buy shares, that might just be an invitation. If you send them money, *you* are making the offer, and they can still say no. This protects sellers from being forced into contracts they aren't ready for yet.
content.academic: Distinguishing between an offer and an invitation to treat is vital for determining when a party is contractually bound. An invitation to treat is a preliminary step in negotiations, described as an 'offer to receive offers.' Unlike an offer, it does not create a power of acceptance. In Berliet Nig. Ltd. v. Francis, the court clarified that a company policy letter regarding share ownership was merely an invitation to treat; the employee's subsequent payment was the actual offer, which the company was free to reject. This distinction ensures that parties are not prematurely bound before a final consensus ad idem is reached.
content.exam: ITT Definition: A preliminary move; an invitation for others to make offers. Legal Effect: No binding obligation. Key Case: Berliet Nig. Ltd. v. Francis.

slides.title: Specific Examples of Invitations to Treat

content.simple: Common daily activities are often misunderstood as offers. 1. Auctions: The auctioneer asking for bids is an invitation. Your bid is the offer, and the contract starts only when the hammer falls. 2. Shop Displays: Items on a supermarket shelf are invitations. You make the offer at the cash register. 3. Catalogues: A price list in a magazine isn't an offer because the seller might run out of stock. 4. Tenders: When a company asks for 'tenders' for a project, they are asking for offers. They can reject even the lowest price because they are just 'inviting' you to bid.
content.academic: Jurisprudence identifies several standard categories of invitations to treat. In auctions, the request for bids is an ITT; the bid is the offer, and acceptance occurs at the fall of the hammer (Payne v. Cave; S.58(2) Sale of Goods Act). Display of goods in self-service shops, as per Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v. Boots Cash Chemists, constitutes an ITT to prevent customers from being bound the moment they pick up an item. Catalogues (Granger & Son v. Gough) and invitations to tender (Spencer v. Harding) are also ITTs, granting the inviter the discretion to select which, if any, offer to accept.
content.exam: Examples: 1. Auctions (Payne v. Cave). 2. Shop Displays (Boots Cash Chemists). 3. Catalogues (Granger v. Gough). 4. Tenders (Spencer v. Harding). Statute: S. 58(2) Sale of Goods Act 1893.

case_references: Pharmaceuticals Society of Great Britain v. Boots Cash Chemists (1953)

sections.title: Consideration in Contract Law>
sections.description: Study of Consideration in Contract Law>

slides.title: Understanding the Nature of Consideration

content.simple: Think of consideration as the 'price' you pay for someone else's promise. In Nigeria, for a contract to be legally binding (unless it is a formal deed under seal), both parties must bring something to the table. It is a two-way street of giving and taking. For example, if you buy a house, your money is the consideration you give, and the house is the consideration the seller gives. It can be a benefit you give to someone or a 'detriment' (a loss or responsibility) you take on yourself. Without this exchange, the law sees the deal as just a gift, which usually cannot be enforced in court.
content.academic: Consideration is an indispensable element in the formation of simple contracts under Nigerian law, representing the 'quid pro quo' or the bargain element. As famously defined by Lush, J. in Currie v. Misa, it consists of either a right, interest, profit, or benefit accruing to one party, or some forbearance, detriment, loss, or responsibility given, suffered, or undertaken by the other. The law requires that for a plaintiff to enforce a promise, they must demonstrate that they have furnished value in the eyes of the law. This value need not be a direct benefit to the promisor, provided the promisee suffers a legal detriment at the promisor's instance.
content.exam: Definition: A benefit to the promisor or detriment to the promisee (Currie v. Misa). Key Rule: Simple contracts are unenforceable without consideration; only contracts under seal are exempt. It must be 'valuable' in the eyes of the law. Case: Currie v. Misa (1875).

key_concepts: Simple Contracts vs. Contracts under Seal

content.simple: The law only cares about things that have actual value, like money, goods, or services. It does not recognize 'sentimental' value. If you promise to pay someone because you love them, or because you feel a moral duty to help them, the Nigerian courts generally won't call that a contract. Even if you feel like you 'owe' it to someone because of past kindness, that is a moral obligation, not legal consideration. While one old Nigerian case suggested love might count, most modern cases follow the rule that love and affection are not enough to make a promise a binding contract.
content.academic: A recurring theme in contract law is the distinction between legal consideration and moral obligation. While Lord Mansfield attempted to equate moral duty with consideration in the 18th century, this was decisively rejected in Eastwood v. Kenyon. The court held that moral obligations do not constitute value in the eye of the law. In Nigeria, although the case of Barclays Bank D.C.O. v. Sulaiman controversially suggested love and affection might suffice, subsequent decisions like Faloughi v. Faloughi have corrected this, aligning Nigerian jurisprudence with the principle that consideration must be objectively valuable and not merely sentimental.
content.exam: Rule: Consideration must be sufficient (of legal value) but need not be adequate. Moral obligation is not consideration (Eastwood v. Kenyon). Love and affection are generally insufficient (Faloughi v. Faloughi).

slides.title: The Rule: Consideration Must Move From Promisee

content.simple: If you want to sue someone for breaking a promise, you must be the one who provided the payment or the service for that promise. You cannot sue on a contract if someone else provided the consideration for you. This is closely tied to 'Privity of Contract,' which means only the people actually involved in the deal can sue. If a political party gives a loan, an individual member cannot sue to get it back because the member didn't personally provide the money. Similarly, a 'gratuitous promise' (a promise for which nothing is given in return) cannot be enforced.
content.academic: The doctrine that consideration must move from the promisee dictates that a party can only enforce a contractual promise if they themselves provided the consideration. This rule ensures that a stranger to the consideration cannot maintain an action. In L.A. Cardoso v. The Executors of the Late J.A. Doherty, the court refused to enforce a promise to allow the plaintiff to live in a house for life because the plaintiff had provided no consideration for that specific promise. Furthermore, as seen in Gbadamosi v. Mbadiwe, if consideration is furnished by a third party (like a political party), an individual plaintiff cannot sue in their personal capacity.
content.exam: Rule: Only the party providing consideration can sue. Linked to Privity of Contract. Cases: L.A. Cardoso v. Doherty (gratuitous promise), Gbadamosi v. Mbadiwe (third-party consideration).

case_references: L.A. Cardoso v. The Executors of the Late J.A. Doherty (1938)

slides.title: Performance and Claims in Excess

content.simple: You cannot win a court case if you haven't done what you promised to do, or if you aren't ready to do it. This is called a 'failure of performance.' Also, if you and a business partner agree on a price, you cannot later demand more money for the same work unless you offer something extra in return. If you try to force a new term into a contract (like a non-compete clause) without giving the other person a new benefit for it, that new term is usually not enforceable because there is no 'extra' consideration for it.
content.academic: Enforceability depends on the plaintiff demonstrating performance or a readiness to perform. In Bank of West Africa v. Fagboyegun, a guarantee failed because the debt existed before the contract and no new funds were advanced, representing a lack of performance. Similarly, any variation of contractual terms must be supported by fresh consideration. In U.T.C v. Hauri, an undertaking by an employee not to compete with his employer was held unenforceable because no extra consideration was furnished for this new promise, and it was obtained under duress. Without a mutual exchange for the variation, the claim is in excess of the original agreement.
content.exam: Performance: Plaintiff must show performance or readiness to perform. Variation: New promises require new consideration. Cases: Bank of West Africa v. Fagboyegun, U.T.C v. Hauri.

case_references: Bank of West Africa v. Fagboyegun (1961)
Ikomi v. Bank of West Africa (1965)

slides.title: Executory, Executed, and Past Consideration

content.simple: There are different 'timings' for consideration. 'Executory' is when both parties trade promises for the future (I'll pay you Friday if you deliver Saturday). 'Executed' is when one person has already finished their part, usually by doing an act (like finding a lost dog to get a reward). However, 'Past Consideration' is usually not allowed. This is when you try to use something you did yesterday as payment for a promise made today. Because that act was already done and wasn't part of the new deal, the law says it doesn't count as valid payment for the new promise.
content.academic: Consideration is classified by timing. Executory consideration consists of mutual promises for future performance, common in bilateral contracts. Executed consideration occurs when one party performs an act in exchange for a promise, typical of unilateral contracts like Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. Conversely, past consideration involves an act performed prior to and independent of the promise in question. Generally, past consideration is no consideration because it was not bargained for in exchange for the current promise. This distinguishes a subsequent expression of gratitude from a legally binding contract.
content.exam: Executory: Promise for a promise (Future). Executed: Act for a promise (Present). Past Consideration: Act done before promise (Invalid). Case: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co.

case_references: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (1893)

sections.title: Intention to Enter Legal Relations>
sections.description: Study of Intention to Enter Legal Relations>

slides.title: Introduction to Intention to Enter Legal Relations

content.simple: Imagine you promise to buy your friend lunch, but you forget. Should your friend be able to sue you in court? Probably not. Contract law says that for a promise to be a real contract, the people involved must actually mean for the law to get involved if someone breaks the promise. This is what we call 'intention to enter legal relations.' Without this intention, a promise is just a social or moral obligation, not a legal one. The courts use 'presumptions'—which are like starting assumptions—to decide if this intention exists depending on whether the deal is between family members or business partners.
content.academic: Intention to create legal relations is a core requirement for contract formation, serving as a filter to distinguish between legally binding obligations and mere social promises. While academics debate its necessity, Nigerian courts adopt an objective approach: if a party's words or conduct suggest they do not wish to be legally bound, no contract arises. This doctrine relies on two rebuttable presumptions. In domestic and social settings, the law presumes parties do not intend legal consequences. Conversely, in commercial transactions, the law presumes a heavy intention to be bound. This objective test ensures that the court does not enforce agreements where the parties never contemplated judicial intervention.
content.exam: Key requirement for enforceability. Objective test based on conduct/words. Two main presumptions: 1. Domestic/Social (presumed no intent). 2. Commercial (presumed intent). These presumptions are rebuttable based on evidence.

content.simple: In family life, people make promises all the time—like a husband promising his wife a monthly allowance. The law assumes these promises are based on love and trust, not legal threats. For example, in the famous Balfour case, a husband promised his wife money while he was away, but when he stopped paying, the court said she couldn't sue him. The court doesn't want to get involved in every small argument between family members, so it starts with the assumption that there is no intention to be legally bound in these personal settings.
content.academic: In domestic and social contexts, there is a strong judicial presumption that parties do not intend to create legal relations. This policy prevents the courts from being overwhelmed by trivial disputes arising from private arrangements. In Balfour v. Balfour, the court established that a husband's promise to pay a maintenance allowance to his wife lacked the requisite animus contrahendi (intention to contract). This principle was further reinforced in cases like Spellman v. Spellman and Jones v. Padavatton, where family arrangements were deemed unenforceable due to the absence of clear evidence that the parties intended to involve the machinery of the law.
content.exam: Presumption: No intention. Cases: Balfour v. Balfour (maintenance), Jones v. Padavatton (parent/child), Spellman v. Spellman. Policy: Avoids over-litigation of domestic life.

content.simple: Even though family promises usually aren't legal, this can change if the situation is serious. If a couple is already separated or getting a divorce, their agreements are likely meant to be legal. Also, if someone makes a huge sacrifice—like selling their house to move in with a relative—the court might step in to protect them. In the Parker case, a couple sold their home to move in with an uncle; because they gave up so much, the court decided the agreement was a binding contract, not just a family favor.
content.academic: The presumption against legal intent in domestic agreements is rebuttable. Courts identify three primary scenarios where the presumption is overturned: 1. When the parties are estranged or separated, as seen in Merritt v. Merritt and McGregor v. McGregor. 2. When the agreement is formalized in writing. 3. When one party has significantly altered their position or incurred 'great sacrifice' in reliance on the promise. In Parker v. Clark, the plaintiffs' decision to sell their own home to move in with defendants constituted sufficient evidence to rebut the domestic presumption, transforming a family arrangement into a legally binding contract.
content.exam: Rebuttal factors: 1. Separation/Estrangement (Merritt v. Merritt). 2. Written agreements. 3. Great sacrifice/Detrimental reliance (Parker v. Clark). 4. Mutual withdrawal of legal actions (McGregor v. McGregor).

content.simple: When you go to a shop or sign a business deal, the law assumes you mean business. If you agree to sell a house for money, you can't later claim you were just joking. In the business world, the starting assumption is the exact opposite of the family world: everyone is presumed to want a legally binding contract. This makes sense because trade would be impossible if people could just walk away from their professional promises without any legal consequences. It is very difficult to convince a judge that a business deal wasn't meant to be legal.
content.academic: In commercial transactions, the law operates under a robust presumption that the parties intend to create legal relations. The burden of proof lies heavily on the party seeking to deny the existence of a contract. This presumption is essential for commercial certainty and the protection of reasonable expectations in the marketplace. For instance, a vendor cannot unilaterally claim a lack of intent after agreeing to a sale price. While this presumption is strong, it remains rebuttable if evidence clearly demonstrates that the parties intended the arrangement to be binding in honour only rather than in law.
content.exam: Presumption: Intent exists. Burden of proof: On the party denying the contract. Purpose: Commercial certainty and stability of trade.

slides.title: Rebutting the Commercial Presumption

content.simple: Sometimes, even in business, there is no legal contract. This happens in two cases: 'mere puffs' and 'honour clauses.' A 'puff' is an obvious exaggeration, like an energy drink saying it gives you wings—no reasonable person would sue over that. However, if a company shows they are serious (like depositing money in a bank to prove it), it becomes a contract, as in the Carbolic Smoke Ball case. An 'honour clause' is when a company explicitly writes in the contract that the agreement is 'not legally binding,' which is very common in football betting pools.
content.academic: The commercial presumption can be rebutted in two ways. First, through 'mere puffs'—exaggerated advertising claims not intended to be taken literally, such as in Weekes v. Tybald. However, if the advertiser demonstrates seriousness through specific actions, as in Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co., the court will find legal intent. Second, through 'honour clauses' or express exclusion clauses. In cases like Amadi v. Pool House Group and Jones v. Vernon's Pools Ltd., the defendants avoided liability by including specific language stating that the agreement was binding in 'honour only,' thereby explicitly ousting the jurisdiction of the courts.
content.exam: Rebuttal methods: 1. Mere Puffs (Weekes v. Tybald vs Carlill). 2. Express Exclusion/Honour Clauses (Amadi v. Pool House Group, Jones v. Vernon's Pools Ltd).

case_references: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (1893)

content.simple: What about deals that aren't quite family but aren't quite business? For example, two neighbors agreeing to take turns driving the kids to school for a small fee. These are called 'intermediate situations.' Because they fall in the middle, the court doesn't use a fixed assumption. Instead, they look at all the facts of the case. In the Coward case, a man paid his colleague for a daily lift to work, but the court decided this was just a friendly arrangement, not a legal contract. In these cases, the judge decides based on how serious the situation looks.
content.academic: Intermediate situations encompass agreements that do not neatly fit into the domestic or commercial categories. In these instances, there is no automatic presumption; the court must determine intention based on a holistic review of the facts and circumstances. In Coward v. Motor Insurers Bureau, the court refused to find a binding contract in a car-pooling arrangement involving weekly payments, viewing it as a social convenience rather than a legal obligation. Similarly, in ACB v. Nwodika, the court emphasized that the contractual effect of agreements in this 'grey area' depends on various contextual factors, requiring a case-by-case judicial determination.
content.exam: No fixed presumption. Case-by-case analysis. Relevant cases: Coward v. Motor Insurers Bureau (car-pooling), ACB v. Nwodika (employment-related factors).

sections.title: Terms of a Contract>
sections.description: Study of Terms of a Contract>

slides.title: Introduction to Contractual Terms and Representations

content.simple: When people enter a contract, they make many statements. Some of these statements are 'terms'—actual promises that form part of the deal. Others are 'representations'—things said just to encourage the other person to sign. Think of it like buying a phone: if the seller promises it has a 2-year warranty, that is likely a term. If they just say 'it looks beautiful,' that is likely a representation. If a term is broken, you can usually sue for more money or even end the contract. If a mere representation is wrong, your legal options are much more limited.
content.academic: Contractual terms constitute the specific obligations parties must fulfill under a valid agreement. It is legally imperative to distinguish between a term and a mere representation, as the breach of a term provides a right to damages and potentially repudiation, whereas a misrepresentation primarily offers rescission. The nature of the term—whether fundamental, a condition, an innominate term, or a warranty—dictates the severity of the breach and the available judicial remedies. Nigerian law follows the common law tradition in using objective tests to determine whether a statement made during negotiations was intended to be a binding contractual term.
content.exam: Term: A binding promise (Damages/Repudiation). Representation: Induces contract but not part of it (Rescission). Key factor: Intention of parties. Case: Bannerman v. White (Importance test).

slides.title: Tests to Distinguish Terms from Representations

content.simple: How do judges decide if a statement is a term? They use five main tests. First, timing: was the statement made right before the contract was signed? Second, expertise: did an expert make the statement to a beginner? Third, writing: was the statement included in the final written document? Fourth, verification: did the speaker tell the other person to double-check the facts? Finally, importance: would the person have walked away if the statement wasn't made? If an expert makes an important statement right before signing and tells you not to worry about checking it, it is almost certainly a term.
content.academic: Courts employ five primary tests to ascertain the status of a statement. The 'Time Test' suggests statements made closer to formation are terms (Routledge v. Mckay). The 'Knowledge Test' holds that statements by parties with superior knowledge are terms (Esso Petroleum v. Mardon). The 'Writing Test' considers if the statement was incorporated into the formal document. Treitel’s 'Verification Test' posits that if a representor discourages verification, it is a term (Ecay v. Godfrey). Lastly, the 'Importance Test' (Bannerman v. Reade) confirms a statement is a term if the representee would not have contracted without it.
content.exam: 1. Time (Routledge v. Mckay); 2. Expertise (Oscar Chess Ltd. v. Williams); 3. Writing (Heilbut v. Buckleton); 4. Verification (Ecay v. Godfrey); 5. Importance (Bannerman).

slides.title: The Doctrine of Collateral Contracts

content.simple: Sometimes, a promise isn't in the main contract, but it's so important that the law treats it as a 'side' contract. This is a collateral contract. Imagine a landlord promises you can keep a pet even though the lease says 'no pets.' If you only signed the lease because of that promise, the promise is a collateral contract. Even if the main contract is written, this side promise (usually oral) can be enforced because the 'payment' for that promise was you agreeing to enter the main deal.
content.academic: A collateral contract is a secondary agreement that exists alongside the main contract, often used to enforce oral promises made during negotiations that induced the party to enter the primary agreement. The consideration for this secondary contract is the act of entering into the main contract. This doctrine is exemplified in City & Westminster Properties Ltd. v. Mudd, where an oral assurance regarding residency overrode a restrictive covenant in the formal lease. It serves as an equitable tool to ensure parties are held to significant promises that fall outside the four corners of the written document.
content.exam: Definition: A side agreement inducing the main contract. Consideration: Entering the main contract. Case: City & Westminster Properties Ltd. v. Mudd.

case_references: City & Westminster Properties Ltd. v. Mudd

content.simple: Not all contract terms are equal. 'Conditions' are the most important—they are the heart of the deal. If a condition is broken, the innocent party can end the contract and sue for damages. 'Warranties' are less important side-promises. If a warranty is broken, you can sue for money (damages), but you still have to finish the contract. Think of a car: the engine working is a condition; the radio working might just be a warranty. There are also 'innominate terms' which sit in the middle, and 'fundamental terms' which are even more vital than conditions.
content.academic: Contractual terms are classified by their gravity. Conditions are essential terms going to the root of the contract; a breach constitutes a total failure of consideration, allowing the innocent party to treat the contract as repudiated. Warranties are collateral or subsidiary terms; their breach only entitles the claimant to damages, not the right to terminate (Section 11(1)(b) Sale of Goods Act 1893). Innominate terms are intermediate; the remedy depends on whether the breach deprived the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract. Fundamental terms represent the core purpose of the agreement.
content.exam: Condition: Root of contract (Repudiation + Damages). Warranty: Subsidiary term (Damages only). Statute: S. 11(1)(b) Sale of Goods Act 1893.

slides.title: Types of Conditions: Precedent and Subsequent

content.simple: In law, 'conditions' can also refer to timing. A 'Condition Precedent' is something that must happen BEFORE the contract even starts—like getting bank approval for a loan. If it doesn't happen, there is no deal. A 'Condition Subsequent' is something that can end an ALREADY existing contract if a certain event happens. For example, in a Nigerian case, a bank's failure to grant a loan after charging valuation fees was seen as a condition that destroyed the obligation. Finally, 'Inherent Conditions' are the basic qualities needed for the contract to exist at all.
content.academic: Conditions are categorized by their operational timing. A Condition Precedent is a prerequisite that must be satisfied before contractual obligations become binding (Pym v. Campbell). A Condition Subsequent is a provision that terminates an existing obligation upon the occurrence of a specific event (African Continental Bank Ltd. v. Okonkwo). Lastly, Conditions Inherent qualify the nature of the obligation itself. The distinction is critical in litigation to determine when a breach occurred or if a binding agreement was ever formed. Nigerian courts strictly apply these distinctions to determine the point of contractual discharge.
content.exam: Precedent: Must be fulfilled for contract to start (Pym v. Campbell). Subsequent: Event that destroys existing obligation (ACB Ltd. v. Okonkwo). Inherent: Qualifies the obligation.

case_references: African Continental Bank Ltd. v. Okonkwo

sections.title: Exclusion Clauses and Limiting Terms>
sections.description: Study of Exclusion Clauses and Limiting Terms>

slides.title: Introduction to Exclusion Clauses and Limiting Terms

content.simple: Imagine you hire a delivery man to move your TV. He gives you a paper saying, 'I am not responsible if the TV breaks.' This is an exclusion clause. It is a part of a contract where one person tries to limit or completely avoid being blamed if something goes wrong. In Nigeria, people have the 'freedom to contract,' meaning you can agree to these terms if you want. These are often found in 'standard form contracts'—those ready-made agreements like the ones you sign when opening a bank account or using an app, where you don't really negotiate the terms.
